perturbation.original_text: "Business Day" means a day other than a Saturday, Sunday or other day on which banks in New York, New York, or San Francisco, California, are authorized or required to close.
perturbation.changed_text: "Business Day" means any day of the week, including Saturday and Sunday, regardless of whether banks in New York, New York, or San Francisco, California, are authorized or required to close.
perturbation.explanation: By redefining 'Business Day' to include weekends and holidays, the contract contradicts standard legal and business practices, potentially affecting deadlines and obligations throughout the agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 6(a)(1) specifies how to compute time periods, excluding weekends and legal holidays when the period is short. The modified definition directly contradicts this rule, which is used in federal court proceedings.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 6. Computing and Extending Time; Time for Motion Papers | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! Г— No thank you Rule 6. Computing and Extending Time; Time for Motion Papers (a) Computing Time. The following rules apply in computing any time period specified in these rules, in any local rule or court order, or in any statute that does not specify a method of computing time. (1) Period Stated in Days or a Longer Unit. When the period is stated in days or a longer unit of time: (A) exclude the day of the event that triggers the period; (B) count every day, including intermediate Saturdays, Sundays, and legal holidays; and (C) include the last day of the period, but if the last day is a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, the period continues to run until the end of the next day that is not a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday. (2) Period Stated in Hours. When the period is stated in hours: (A) begin counting immediately on the occurrence of the event that triggers the period; (B) count every hour, including hours during intermediate Saturdays, Sundays, and legal holidays; and (C) if the period would end on a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday, the period continues to run until the same time on the next day that is not a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday. (3) Inaccessibility of the Clerk's Office. Unless the court orders otherwise, if the clerk's office is inaccessible: (A) on the last day for filing under Rule 6(a)(1) , then the time for filing is extended to the first accessible day that is not a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday; or (B) during the last hour for filing under Rule 6(a)(2) , then the time for filing is extended to the same time on the first accessible day that is not a Saturday, Sunday, or legal holiday. (4) вҖңLast DayвҖқ Defined. Unless a different time is set by a statute, local rule, or court order, the last day ends: (A) for electronic filing, at midnight in the court's time zone; and (B) for filing by other means, when the clerk's office is scheduled to close. (5) вҖңNext DayвҖқ Defined. The вҖңnext dayвҖқ is determined by continuing to count forward when the period is measured after an event and backward when measured before an event. (6) вҖңLegal HolidayвҖқ Defined. вҖңLegal holidayвҖқ means: (A) the day set aside by statute for observing New Year's Day, Martin Luther King Jr.'s Birthday, Washington's Birthday, Memorial Day, Juneteen
perturbation.justification: The changed text redefines 'Business Day' to include weekends and holidays, directly contradicting the standard legal practice, especially concerning deadlines. FRCP 6(a)(1), as shown in Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifies excluding weekends and legal holidays when calculating time periods, directly clashing with the new definition. This contradiction falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition, as the contractual language deviates from legally recognized definitions, potentially making the contract unenforceable. The original definition aligns with the legal standard, while the modified version explicitly defies it.

perturbation.original_text: Operator shall establish and maintain an adequate spare parts inventory, to be located at one or both Sites to serve exclusively the Facilities.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator may, at its discretion, maintain a spare parts inventory, which may be located anywhere and may be used for any purpose.
perturbation.explanation: This change makes the spare parts inventory discretionary and removes the requirement for it to be dedicated to the Facilities, potentially violating the implied duty to maintain the systems, and also opening up the possibility of not having the materials needed to maintain the energy output.
perturbation.law_explanation: Although the contract disclaims being a sale of goods, this section of the Delaware UCC imposes a duty of good faith in requirements contracts. If the lack of spare parts hinders the provision of services, it could be argued that the operator isn't acting in good faith, particularly if the original agreement implied a certain level of commitment to maintaining the facilities.

The obligation of the seller is to transfer and deliver and that of the buyer is to accept and pay in accordance with the contract. 5A Del. C. 1953, ГӮВ§ГӮВ§ ГўВҖВӮ2-301;ГӮ 55 Del. Laws, c. 349. ;ГӮ ГӮВ§ 2-302. Unconscionable contract or clause. (1) If the court as a matter of law finds the contract or any clause of the contract to have been unconscionable at the time it was made the court may refuse to enforce the contract, or it may enforce the remainder of the contract without the unconscionable clause, or it may so limit the application of any unconscionable clause as to avoid any unconscionable result. (2) When it is claimed or appears to the court that the contract or any clause thereof may be unconscionable the parties shall be afforded a reasonable opportunity to present evidence as to its commercial setting, purpose and effect to aid the court in making the determination. 5A Del. C. 1953, ГӮВ§ГӮВ§ ГўВҖВӮ2-302;ГӮ 55 Del. Laws, c. 349. ;ГӮ ГӮВ§ 2-303. Allocation or division of risks. Where this Article allocates a risk or a burden as between the parties ГўВҖВңunless otherwise agreedГўВҖВқ, the agreement may not only shift the allocation but may also divide the risk or burden. 5A Del. C. 1953, ГӮВ§ГӮВ§ ГўВҖВӮ2-303;ГӮ 55 Del. Laws, c. 349. ;ГӮ ГӮВ§ 2-304. Price payable in money, goods, realty, or otherwise. (1) The price can be made payable in money or otherwise. If it is payable in whole or in part in goods each party is a seller of the goods which he or she is to transfer. perturbation.justification: The original contract mandates the Operator to maintain a spare parts inventory at the specified sites for the facilities. The changed text gives the operator the discretion to maintain the inventory, to locate it anywhere, and to use it for any purpose. This directly contradicts the original obligation. The Law Explanation suggests this alteration could conflict with the duty of good faith, especially concerning requirements contracts under Delaware UCC. Scraped Law Snippet 1 includes ГӮВ§ 2-301 which states the general obligations of parties. The change removes the requirement to maintain an adequate spare parts inventory at the specified locations, which is a core obligation under the original contract. The change creates a contradiction because it changes a mandated obligation to a discretionary one.

perturbation.original_text: Owner agrees that Operator shall retain all rights, title and interest, including intellectual property rights, in any Training Materials in connection with the services performed hereunder. Operator grants to Owner the limited right to use any Training Materials which are provided under this Agreement, and Owner agrees that upon termination of this Agreement for any reason, Owner shall return all Training Materials, including any copies, to Operator. Owner will not make copies nor will it permit its employees, contractors, affiliates, or representatives to make copies of any Training Materials without Operator's prior written consent.
perturbation.changed_text: Operator retains ownership of Training Materials but grants Owner an irrevocable, perpetual, worldwide, royalty-free license to use, modify, and distribute the Training Materials, even after termination of this agreement.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates a contradiction. The original clause states that the owner should return all Training Materials. The updated clause states that the owner has the right to use, modify and distribute the training materials, even after termination.
perturbation.law_explanation: The original language aligns with standard IP ownership, but the modified language implies a transfer of rights akin to ownership, potentially conflicting with copyright law if the 'license' isn't properly defined or if it effectively negates the Operator's copyright. An irrevocable license can only be granted by the copyright owner.

perturbation.justification: The original contract specifies that the owner must return all training materials upon termination. The modified contract grants the owner an irrevocable, perpetual license to use, modify, and distribute these materials, even after termination, directly contradicting the original requirement to return them. The Law Explanation states that this "implies a transfer of rights akin to ownership", which conflicts with the original IP ownership and copyright. Scraped Law Snippet 1 shows incomprehensible text.
